text: Lesson 4.4: Applications to Physics – Work, Center of Mass, and Probability

body: The work done by a force that varies with position is defined as the integral \(W=\int_{a}^{b}F(x)\,dx\).  For a spring obeying Hooke’s law \(F(x)=k\,x\), the work required to stretch the spring from \(x=a\) to \(x=b\) becomes \(W=\frac{k}{2}(b^{2}-a^{2})\), illustrating how integration replaces the simple \(F\cdot d\) product when the force is non‑constant.

text: Key Takeaway:  Work is the signed area under the force‑versus‑displacement curve; the integral automatically accounts for direction and varying magnitude.

items: Determine the limits of motion \(a\) and \(b\).
Evaluate the integral analytically or numerically.

text: Center of Mass for Laminae and Solids of Revolution

body: For a thin plate of uniform density \(\rho\) occupying region \(R\), the centroid \((\bar x,\bar y)\) is given by \(\bar x=\frac{1}{A}\iint_R x\,dA\) and \(\bar y=\frac{1}{A}\iint_R y\,dA\), where \(A\) is the area.  When the region is revolved about an axis, the solid’s center of mass uses the same moment formulas but with volume elements \(dV=\pi [f(x)]^{2}\,dx\).

items: Solid of revolution (about x‑axis): \(\bar x = \frac{\int_a^b x\,\pi[f(x)]^2\,dx}{\int_a^b \pi[f(x)]^2\,dx}\)

title: Example: Centroid of a Triangular Plate
body: Consider the right triangle with vertices (0,0), (4,0), (0,3).  Using \(y = -\frac{3}{4}x + 3\) as the upper boundary, \(A=\frac{1}{2}(4)(3)=6\).  Compute \(\bar x = \frac{1}{6}\int_0^4 x\,\big( -\frac{3}{4}x + 3\big)\,dx = \frac{4}{3}\) and \(\bar y = \frac{1}{6}\int_0^4 \frac{1}{2}\big( -\frac{3}{4}x + 3\big)^2\,dx = \frac{1}{2}\).  The centroid lies at \((4/3, 1/2)\).

A probability density function \(f(x)\) must satisfy \(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1\).  When \(f(x)\) is defined on an infinite interval or has singularities, the integral is improper and evaluated as a limit.  For example, the normalized Cauchy density \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}\) uses the limit \(\lim_{R\to\infty}\int_{-R}^{R} f(x)\,dx = 1\).

title: Work – Lamar University
relevance: Provides the formal definition of work as an integral and a step‑by‑step example with a variable force.

title: Center of Mass – Lamar University
relevance: Derives centroid formulas for laminae and solids of revolution, matching the calculations presented.

title: Improper Integrals and Probability Density Functions
relevance: Explains how improper integrals are used to normalize continuous probability distributions.

questions.q: A spring with constant \(k=150\,\text{N/m}\) is compressed from \(x=0.2\,\text{m}\) to \(x=0.5\,\text{m}\).  What integral gives the work done?
questions.explanation: Hooke’s law gives \(F(x)=kx\); the work is \(\int_{0.2}^{0.5}kx\,dx\).

questions.q: For the triangular plate in the example, which expression correctly computes \(\bar y\)?
questions.explanation: The moment about the x‑axis uses \(\frac{1}{2}y^2\) as the integrand.

questions.q: Which limit correctly normalizes the Cauchy density \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}\)?
questions.explanation: The integral is improper on both sides, so we take the symmetric limit.

questions.q: When revolving the region under \(y=\sqrt{x}\) from \(0\) to \(4\) about the x‑axis, the x‑coordinate of the solid’s center of mass is given by:
questions.explanation: The volume element is \(dV=\pi (\sqrt{x})^2\,dx = \pi x\,dx\); the moment uses \(x\,dV\).

title: Interactive Visualization: Spring Work & Triangle Centroid
caption: Use the left pane to adjust the spring constant \(k\) and limits \(a,b\) to see work change.  The right pane plots a triangle and its medians, highlighting the centroid at \((\bar x,\bar y)\).

title: Ask the Tutor
greeting: Hello! I'm Dr. Maya Patel, a theoretical physicist. I can help you see how these integrals tie into energy conservation, statistical ensembles, and the deeper physics behind work, mass distribution, and probability.

text: Essay Prompt:  Critique the choice of integration method (direct integral, polar coordinates, or symmetry arguments) for each of the three physical scenarios presented.  Discuss accuracy, computational efficiency, and conceptual clarity.
